explanation: The rule uses literal string matching (CommandLine|contains) for suspicious paths like '\AppData\Local\Temp\' and '\Users\Public\'. By using environment variables (%TEMP%, %USERPROFILE%), the command line does not contain these literal strings, evading the string match, while the OS expands them to the same suspicious directories at runtime. Mutation type: Recoding/Encoding (environment variable substitution).

The rule checks for literal path strings like ':\Windows\Temp\'. By appending '\..\Temp\' (directory traversal that resolves to the same path), the literal string ':\Windows\Temp\' is broken in the command line, but the filesystem resolves it to the same target directory. Mutation type: Insertion (path normalization trickery).

The rule looks for ':\Temp\' but the actual system temp directory is often 'C:\Temp' (short path) or a junction. Using 'C:\TEMP\' (case variation) or the real short path 'C:\TEMP' may not match the literal ':\Temp\' pattern if the rule expects a drive
